content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 17 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 108r–109v | **Source Images:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/DEU_017_page_001.jpg, DEU_017_page_002.jpg | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), p. 154

---

1 You shall not sacrifice to the LORD your God an ox or a sheep in which is a blemish, any defect whatever, for that is an abomination to the LORD your God.

2 If there is found among you, within any of your gates that the LORD your God is giving you, a man or woman who does what is evil in the sight of the LORD your God, in transgressing his covenant,

3 and has gone and served other gods and worshiped them — or the sun or the moon or any of the host of heaven, which I have not commanded —

4 and it is told to you and you hear of it, then you shall inquire diligently, and if it is true and certain that such an abomination has been done in Israel,

5 then you shall bring out to your gates that man or that woman who has done this evil thing, and you shall stone that man or that woman to death with stones.

6 On the evidence of two witnesses or three witnesses the one who is to die shall be put to death; he shall not be put to death on the evidence of one witness.

7 The hand of the witnesses shall be first against him to put him to death, and afterward the hand of all the people. So you shall purge the evil from your midst.

8 If any case is too difficult for you to decide, between one kind of bloodshed and another, between one kind of legal right and another, or between one kind of assault and another — any case within your gates that is too hard for you — then you shall arise and go up to the place that the LORD your God will choose.

9 And you shall come to the Levitical priests and to the judge who is in office in those days, and you shall inquire, and they shall declare to you the decision of the case.

10 Then you shall do according to the decision that they declare to you from that place that the LORD will choose, and you shall be careful to do according to all that they direct you.

11 According to the instruction that they give you, and according to the decision that they declare to you, you shall do. You shall not turn aside from the decision that they declare to you, to the right or to the left.

12 And the man who acts presumptuously by not listening to the priest who stands to minister there before the LORD your God, or to the judge, that man shall die. So you shall purge the evil from Israel.

13 And all the people shall hear and fear and shall not act presumptuously again.

14 When you come to the land that the LORD your God is giving you, and you possess it and dwell in it and say: "I will set a king over me, like all the nations that are around me,"

15 you shall indeed set a king over you whom the LORD your God will choose. From among your brothers you shall set a king over you; you may not put a foreigner over you, who is not your brother.

16 Only he shall not acquire many horses for himself or cause the people to return to Egypt in order to acquire many horses, since the LORD has said to you: "You shall never return that way again."

17 And he shall not acquire many wives for himself, lest his heart turn away; nor shall he acquire for himself excessive silver and gold.

18 And when he sits on the throne of his kingdom, he shall write for himself a copy of this instruction in a book, from that which is before the Levitical priests.

19 And it shall be with him, and he shall read in it all the days of his life, so that he may learn to fear the LORD his God by keeping all the words of this instruction and these statutes, to do them,

20 that his heart may not be lifted up above his brothers, and that he may not turn aside from the commandment to the right or to the left, so that he may continue long in his kingdom, he and his children, in Israel.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

**Verse 1 — kol davar ra' (any defect whatever):** "Any defect whatever" for *kol davar ra'* (lit. "any bad/evil thing"). The language is deliberately broad. The connection to the judicial rules following (vv. 2–7) may be intentional: blemished offerings and people who transgress the covenant are linked by the concept of *to'evah* (abomination). LXX: *en auto momos* ("a blemish in it").

**Verse 3 — tzeva ha-shamayim (the host of heaven):** "The host of heaven" for *tzeva ha-shamayim* — the celestial bodies as objects of veneration. Astral worship was a major concern of the Deuteronomic reformers. LXX: *ten strategian tou ouranou* ("the army of heaven").

**Verse 6 — al pi shnayim 'edim (on the evidence of two witnesses):** "On the evidence of two witnesses or three witnesses" for *al pi shnayim 'edim o shloshah 'edim*. The two-or-three-witness requirement is a foundational principle of biblical jurisprudence (Num 35:30; Deut 19:15; Matt 18:16; 2 Cor 13:1). LXX: *epi duo martyrsin kai epi trisin martyrsin* — exact.

**Verse 8 — din le-din (one kind of legal right and another):** "Between one kind of legal right and another" for *bein din le-din* — civil lawsuit vs. civil lawsuit. The "too hard" cases (*yipaleh mimmekha*) represent the limits of local adjudication. LXX: *ana meson haima haimati kai ana meson krisis kriseos* — "between blood and blood, between judgment and judgment."

**Verses 9, 12 — ha-kohen ha-levi'im (the Levitical priests):** "The Levitical priests" for *ha-kohanim ha-levi'im* — the characteristic Deuteronomic phrase uniting priestly and Levitical identity. LXX: *tous hiereis tous Leuittas* — exact.

**Verse 12 — be-zadon (presumptuously):** "Presumptuously" for *zadon* — willful arrogance or presumption in defying the court. The same root (*zid/zur*) underlies the "presumptuous sin" of Ps 19:13. LXX: *hyperephania* ("arrogance").

**Verse 18 — mishneh ha-torah (a copy of this instruction):** The critical phrase from which the Greek title *Deuteronomion* ("second law") is derived. The LXX renders *to Deuteronomion touto* — "this second law." LOGOS renders *mishneh ha-torah* as "a copy of this instruction," following the most defensible MT interpretation: *mishneh* means a copy/duplicate (not "second" in the ordinal sense of a different law), and *torah* here means this specific body of instruction. The king's personal torah scroll is both a constraint on royal power and a model for Israel's covenant life.

**Verse 19 — yare' (to fear):** "To fear the LORD his God" — the same *yirat YHWH* formula that runs throughout Deuteronomy. The king's reading of the torah is not ceremonial but formative: it shapes the heart toward the fear of YHWH as the foundation of covenant faithfulness.

**Verse 20 — lo yarum levavo (his heart may not be lifted up):** "His heart may not be lifted up above his brothers" — the law of the king is fundamentally egalitarian: the monarch is *ach* (brother) to his subjects, not lord over them. This democratizing of royal identity is unique in the ancient Near East. LXX: *hina me hypsothei he kardia autou* — "so that his heart may not be lifted up."

**Verse 20 — le-ma'an ya'arich yamim (that he may continue long):** "That he may continue long in his kingdom" for *le-ma'an ya'arich yamim*. The length of days on the land is tied to covenant obedience throughout Deuteronomy (cf. 4:40; 5:16; 6:2). LXX: *hina poluchrone basileuon* — "so that he reigns for many years."

*— LOGOS*
